verses.text: When the work is done, God appears to Solomon again, promising that if he walks before Him as David did, his throne will be established for ever. The greater the privilege, the greater the call to faithfulness. God's blessings come with the charge to keep walking with Him.

But if Solomon or his sons turn to other gods, God will cut off Israel and cast out this house, making it 'a proverb and a byword.' The glorious temple is no charm against apostasy. Privilege presumed upon and faith abandoned bring ruin, however sacred the place.

Solomon gives Hiram twenty Galilean cities for his help, but Hiram is displeased and calls them 'Cabul,' good for nothing. Even between allies, dealings can sour. The friendships of this world, built on exchange, are fragile; only fellowship grounded in God endures.

Solomon's great levy builds the temple, his palace, the walls of Jerusalem, and store and chariot cities across the land. The reign is one of vast construction and strength. God had given rest, and he used it to build — yet the heavy levy also sowed seeds of later discontent.

The remnant of the old nations are put to bondservice, but Israelites serve as soldiers and officers, not slaves. The distinction is carefully kept. God's people, redeemed from bondage, were not to be reduced to it again; freedom won by grace is to be guarded.

Pharaoh's daughter moves to her own house, and Solomon offers sacrifices three times a year on the altar he built. Worship continues its ordered round. Yet the foreign marriage, noted in passing, is a quiet shadow over a reign that such alliances will one day undo.

Solomon builds a navy at Ezion-geber, and Hiram's seamen bring back great gold from Ophir. The wealth of distant lands flows to Jerusalem. The LORD prospered the king who began by seeking wisdom — yet riches, like power, would test whether his heart stayed true.
